instructions.shared: Four facts about ML that content must not contradict or omit: (1) the goal of training is generalization to unseen data, not performance on the training set; (2) data quality and preparation cause more real-world ML failures than algorithm choice; (3) accuracy alone is unreliable on imbalanced datasets and should not be treated as the default success metric; (4) models degrade in production when the data distribution changes from training time — this is expected, not a bug. Beyond these, the AI has full latitude over framing, narrative, pacing, and presentation style.
instructions.planning:
Use the ml-depth-guide document to assign depth levels to subtopics. The areas most courses under-teach — and where learners form the most persistent wrong mental models — are data leakage, the accuracy trap, overfitting diagnosis, and the bias-variance tradeoff. These deserve critical-depth treatment and should not be compressed.

One hard prerequisite dependency: data preparation must appear before model evaluation, and model evaluation before hyperparameter tuning. All other ordering is at the curriculum planner's discretion.

Two topics commonly absent from beginner syllabi but worth including at light depth: (1) distribution shift — why models degrade in production when the statistical properties of incoming data change from training time; (2) model calibration — whether a model's predicted probabilities reflect the actual frequency of outcomes, relevant when the probability value itself drives a decision.

Problem definition: the first lesson of any ML course should spend at least some time on whether ML is the right tool at all. Many real problems are better solved with simpler approaches. Beginners who skip this step write solutions before understanding what they are solving.

Project timing: research on ML pedagogy shows students engage with theory more effectively after hands-on experience. A working model should appear early — before all algorithms have been explained — not only at the end.
instructions.lesson:
Minimum coverage requirements by topic area — these are floors, not templates:

Data prep topics: the lesson must address what goes wrong if the step is skipped or done in the wrong order, not just how to perform it. Leakage in particular requires a concrete broken-pipeline example, not just a definition.

Algorithm topics: the lesson must cover the type of problem the algorithm solves, the core mechanism, and at least one meaningful limitation or failure case. The limitation is not optional.

Metrics topics: any classification metric lesson must include a worked example with a concrete confusion matrix using real numbers. Formulas alone are insufficient.

Optimization topics: a lesson on gradient descent must convey why learning rate sensitivity matters, not just define the update rule.

Code: when code examples appear, use Python and scikit-learn with the fit/predict pattern as the assumed interface.
instructions.agent:
When asked which algorithm to use: reason from the constraints of the problem — target type, dataset size, interpretability requirement, metric that matters — rather than giving an unqualified direct answer.

When a misconception is present in a question: correct it before answering the rest of the question. ML misconceptions compound and should not be left implicit.

When comparing models: never describe one as better than another without naming the dimension — accuracy on this problem type, interpretability, training cost, inference latency, or data requirements.
instructions.quiz:
Questions must test the learner's ability to reason about ML, not retrieve definitions. A question that can be answered correctly by quoting a textbook sentence is not a useful ML quiz question.

The highest-value topics to assess with judgment questions: the accuracy trap on imbalanced data, data leakage detection, overfitting vs. underfitting diagnosis, and metric selection for domain-specific problems.

For any question involving classification metrics: include the class distribution or confusion matrix so the learner can reason from concrete numbers, not abstractions.
instructions.recall: Recall cues should present a situation the learner must evaluate or diagnose — not a definition to complete. The highest-value concepts to build ML recall around: accuracy trap, data leakage, overfitting diagnosis, bias-variance tradeoff, metric selection, and the test-set rule. Specific scenario, difficulty, and framing are at the AI's discretion.

documents.title: Depth assignment guide for ML topics
documents.content:
DEPTH ASSIGNMENT GUIDE — Machine Learning Foundations

Critical (5–8 pages): full treatment, failure modes, edge cases.
- Train/test/validation split and the rationale for three-way separation
- Data leakage: target leakage and train-test contamination
- Class imbalance and its effect on every classification metric
- Overfitting and underfitting (demonstrate with real numbers)
- Bias-variance tradeoff
- k-fold and stratified cross-validation
- Confusion matrix and all derived metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1, ROC-AUC, PR-AUC)
- The accuracy trap on imbalanced datasets
- Decision trees: splitting criteria (Gini, entropy), information gain, pruning
- Random forests: bagging, random feature subsets, OOB error, feature importance

Important (3–5 pages): explain the mechanism; recurring concept.
- Linear regression, OLS objective, geometric interpretation
- Regularization: Ridge (L2) and Lasso (L1)
- Logistic regression and the sigmoid function
- Feature scaling and why optimization depends on it
- Gradient descent, learning rate, convergence
- Hyperparameter tuning (grid search, random search)
- Learning curves: diagnosing overfitting vs underfitting
- Scikit-learn Pipeline: preventing leakage in cross-validation
- Feature engineering basics

Medium (2–3 pages): explain the concept, one or two examples.
- What ML is and why it exists
- kNN and Naive Bayes intuition and when to use them
- SVM margin and kernel trick intuitively
- Gradient boosting overview
- k-means, DBSCAN, hierarchical clustering
- PCA intuition and use cases
- SHAP and LIME conceptually
- Backpropagation intuition
- MLP architecture

Medium (2–3 pages): explain the concept, one or two examples.
- Model calibration: what calibrated probabilities mean, reliability diagrams, Brier score, when calibration matters

Light (1–2 pages): define and move on.
- Types of ML overview
- Reinforcement learning (conceptual only)
- t-SNE and UMAP
- Saving/loading models with joblib
- Experiment tracking overview
- Distribution shift and model degradation in production: covariate shift, concept drift, why 91% of models degrade, what retraining means

documents.title: Data preparation — the non-negotiable section
documents.content:
DATA PREPARATION — THE NON-NEGOTIABLE SECTION

This is where most ML courses under-teach and where most real ML projects fail. Treat it with the same rigor as model evaluation.

Train/val/test split: must be done before any preprocessing. The most common beginner mistake is scaling before splitting — this causes data leakage. Always show the split-then-scale order explicitly.

Data leakage: two forms. (1) Target leakage: a feature is derived from the target or becomes available only after the target is known (e.g., 'days in hospital' when predicting readmission). (2) Train-test contamination: a transformer (scaler, imputer, encoder) is fit on the full dataset. The test set bleeds into training. Always test understanding with a concrete broken pipeline.

Missing values: imputation strategy depends on why values are missing. Mean/median imputation for MCAR. More careful handling for MAR/MNAR. The imputer must always be fit only on training data.

Feature scaling: StandardScaler and MinMaxScaler. Required for: kNN, SVM, linear models, neural networks. Not required for: tree-based models. Teach scaling as conditionally necessary, not always necessary.

Categorical encoding: one-hot (drop_first to avoid dummy variable trap), ordinal (when order exists), target encoding (risk of leakage — needs cross-fitting).

Class imbalance: a 99/1 split allows 'predict majority always' to score 99% accuracy. Remedies: class_weight parameter, SMOTE oversampling, threshold adjustment, PR-AUC as the metric.

Temporal leakage (time-series data): when data has a time dimension, randomly shuffling and splitting creates leakage — future values end up in the training set. Standard k-fold cross-validation is wrong for sequences. Always use time-based splits: train on all data before date T, validate on data after T. Feature engineering must use only information available at actual prediction time.

Core lesson to reinforce: bad data makes a good algorithm useless. The pipeline order matters as much as the algorithm choice.

documents.title: Supervised learning — regression algorithms and metrics
documents.content:
SUPERVISED LEARNING — REGRESSION

Open with a concrete prediction problem (house prices, temperature) before defining regression. Target is a continuous numeric value.

Linear regression: weight × feature + bias. The OLS objective minimizes sum of squared residuals. Teach the geometric interpretation (line of best fit in 2D, hyperplane in nD) before the algebraic form. Key: linear in the parameters, not necessarily in the features.

Polynomial regression: add engineered features (x², x³) to capture non-linear patterns. Natural bridge to overfitting — higher degree = lower training error, higher variance.

Regularization:
- Ridge (L2): adds λ × Σw² to the loss. Shrinks all weights toward zero but never exactly to zero. Use when all features may matter.
- Lasso (L1): adds λ × Σ|w| to the loss. Drives some weights to exactly zero — built-in feature selection. Use when many features are likely irrelevant.
- λ is the dial between underfitting (λ too high) and overfitting (λ too low).

Evaluation metrics:
- MAE: mean absolute error. Same units as target. Robust to outliers.
- MSE: penalizes large errors quadratically. Sensitive to outliers.
- RMSE: square root of MSE. Same units as target. Most common default.
- R²: proportion of variance explained. 0 = predicts the mean (baseline), 1 = perfect fit, negative = worse than the baseline. Do not describe R² as 'accuracy' — it is a relative goodness-of-fit measure and cannot be compared across different targets.

documents.title: Supervised learning — classification algorithms
documents.content:
SUPERVISED LEARNING — CLASSIFICATION ALGORITHMS

Logistic regression: despite the name, it is a classifier. Outputs P(class=1) via sigmoid(w·x + b). Decision boundary is linear. Fast, interpretable, calibrated probabilities. Use as the first baseline before any complex model.

kNN: no training phase — classifies by majority vote of k nearest neighbors. Low k = high variance. High k = high bias. Requires feature scaling. Slow at inference for large datasets. Useful for intuition, not production.

Naive Bayes: assumes feature independence (often violated but works well for text). Computes P(class|features) ∝ P(class) × ∏P(feature|class). Very fast, good for high-dimensional sparse data (text, bag-of-words).

Decision trees: recursive binary splits chosen to maximize information gain (entropy) or minimize Gini impurity. Highly interpretable — each path from root to leaf is an if-then rule. Prone to overfitting without depth limits or pruning.

Random forests: bagging (bootstrap sampling) + random feature subsets per split. Each tree sees a different random view of the data. Aggregated vote reduces variance dramatically. Feature importance built-in via mean decrease in impurity.

Gradient boosting (XGBoost, LightGBM): additive model — each new tree corrects the residuals of the ensemble so far. More accurate than random forests on tabular data. Requires careful tuning (learning rate, depth, n_estimators) to avoid overfitting.

SVM: finds the maximum-margin hyperplane between classes. Kernel trick maps data to higher dimensions to find linear separability. Effective in high-dimensional spaces. Computationally expensive for large datasets.

Teach order: logistic → decision tree → random forest → boosting. Introduce SVM and kNN as conceptual contrast points.

documents.title: Classification metrics — the accuracy trap
documents.content:
CLASSIFICATION METRICS — THE ACCURACY TRAP

Open with the class imbalance scenario: fraud detection with 1% positive rate. A model that always predicts 'not fraud' scores 99% accuracy and is completely useless. This is the single most important lesson in classification evaluation.

Confusion matrix — always show with real numbers and labeled axes:
- True Positives (TP): predicted positive, actually positive
- True Negatives (TN): predicted negative, actually negative
- False Positives (FP): predicted positive, actually negative (Type I error)
- False Negatives (FN): predicted negative, actually positive (Type II error)

Derived metrics:
- Accuracy = (TP + TN) / total. Misleading when classes are imbalanced.
- Precision = TP / (TP + FP). 'Of those we flagged, how many were actually positive?'
- Recall = TP / (TP + FN). 'Of all actual positives, how many did we catch?'
- F1 = 2 × (Precision × Recall) / (Precision + Recall). Harmonic mean. Use when both precision and recall matter.
- ROC-AUC: area under the TPR vs FPR curve across all classification thresholds. Robust to class imbalance in evaluation, but not ideal for highly skewed data.
- PR-AUC: area under precision-recall curve. Better than ROC-AUC when positives are rare.

The precision-recall tradeoff: lowering the decision threshold increases recall (catch more positives) but decreases precision (more false alarms). Which to optimize depends on the cost of each error type. Cancer screening = maximize recall. Spam filter = maximize precision.

Rule: never report only accuracy on a classification problem. Always show the confusion matrix.

documents.title: Model evaluation and validation
documents.content:
MODEL EVALUATION AND VALIDATION

This must be a major standalone chapter, not an appendix to algorithm chapters.

Overfitting: the model memorizes training data and fails on new data. Signs: training error near zero, validation error much higher. Root causes: model too complex for the dataset size, insufficient regularization.

Underfitting: model too simple to capture the underlying pattern. Signs: both training and validation error are high. Root causes: wrong model family, too few features, excessive regularization.

Bias-variance tradeoff: high bias → underfitting (model ignores real patterns). High variance → overfitting (model chases noise). The tradeoff cannot be eliminated, only managed through model selection and regularization.

Cross-validation: split data into k folds. Train on k-1 folds, validate on the held-out fold. Rotate k times, average the k validation scores. More reliable than a single train/val split — especially on small datasets. Stratified k-fold preserves class ratios per fold.

Hyperparameter tuning: use validation data (or cross-validation) to select hyperparameters. The test set is touched exactly once — after all tuning is complete. Using test set performance to guide tuning is data leakage.

Learning curves: plot training and validation error vs. training set size. Large gap between curves = overfitting (needs more data or regularization). Both curves high = underfitting (needs better model or features).

Baselines: always compare against a naive baseline before claiming a model works. Majority class classifier for classification. Mean predictor for regression. A model that does not beat the baseline is not a model.

Error analysis: inspect failures. Cluster them by type. Find the patterns. This is where real model improvement happens.

Calibration: a well-calibrated model's predicted probability reflects the actual frequency of the outcome. If a model outputs 0.80 for a set of predictions, roughly 80% of those should actually be positive. Most classifiers are not well-calibrated by default — tree-based models tend to be overconfident, logistic regression tends to be better. Calibration matters when the probability value itself drives a decision (medical risk scores, lending). Reliability diagrams (calibration curves) visualize it. Brier score measures it numerically. Teach as light awareness, not deep implementation.

documents.title: Optimization basics — loss functions and gradient descent
documents.content:
OPTIMIZATION BASICS

Students need the mechanism, not the proofs. Core understanding: training means adjusting parameters to reduce a loss function.

Loss functions:
- MSE loss for regression: L = (1/n) Σ(y - ŷ)². Penalizes large errors quadratically.
- Cross-entropy loss for classification: L = -Σ y·log(ŷ). Penalizes confident wrong predictions very heavily. A model that says 99% probability for the wrong class pays a very large penalty.

Gradient descent intuition: the loss is a landscape of hills and valleys. The gradient at any point is the direction of steepest ascent. Move opposite to the gradient (downhill). Repeat until the loss stops decreasing or a minimum is reached.

Update rule: θ ← θ - α × ∇L(θ)
- θ: model parameters (weights and biases)
- α: learning rate (step size)
- ∇L: gradient of the loss with respect to parameters

Learning rate sensitivity:
- Too high: overshoots the minimum, loss oscillates or diverges
- Too low: converges very slowly, may get stuck in a shallow plateau
- Learning rate is often the first hyperparameter to tune

Variants:
- Batch GD: full dataset per update. Stable gradient, slow per iteration.
- Stochastic GD (SGD): one sample per update. Fast but noisy gradient.
- Mini-batch GD: 32–256 samples per update. Balances speed and stability. Standard in practice.

Why feature scaling matters for optimization: if features have very different scales, the loss landscape is a narrow elongated valley — gradient descent zigzags. Scaled features make the landscape more spherical and convergence faster.

documents.title: Unsupervised learning — clustering and dimensionality reduction
documents.content:
UNSUPERVISED LEARNING

Core distinction: no labels. The algorithm finds structure in unlabeled data. Evaluation is harder because there is often no single correct answer.

Clustering:

k-means: assigns points to k centroids by Euclidean distance. Iteratively updates centroids until convergence. Requires pre-specifying k — use the elbow method (inertia vs k) or silhouette score to select it. Assumes spherical clusters of similar density. Fails on non-convex shapes, unequal cluster sizes.

DBSCAN: density-based. Does not require specifying k. Finds clusters of arbitrary shape. Labels low-density points as noise (outliers). Two hyperparameters: ε (neighborhood radius) and min_samples. Robust to outliers but sensitive to hyperparameter choice.

Hierarchical clustering: builds a dendrogram by iteratively merging nearest clusters (agglomerative). Does not require k upfront — cut the dendrogram at any height. Good for visualizing cluster structure. Expensive for large datasets.

Evaluation: silhouette score measures how well each point fits its assigned cluster vs. neighboring clusters. Range: -1 to +1, higher is better. Do not use the objective the algorithm minimizes (e.g., k-means inertia) as the evaluation metric — it always improves as k increases.

Dimensionality reduction:

PCA: linear projection onto principal components (directions of maximum variance). Use for: removing correlated features, compression, visualization, preprocessing before distance-based algorithms. Components are not interpretable in original feature space.

t-SNE: non-linear. Excellent for 2D/3D visualization of high-dimensional data. Do not use for feature engineering — stochastic, distance relationships not globally preserved.

UMAP: similar purpose to t-SNE but faster, better global structure preservation.

documents.title: Feature importance and model interpretability
documents.content:
FEATURE IMPORTANCE AND INTERPRETABILITY

A model can be accurate and still misleading. Interpretability reveals what the model learned and where it may fail.

Linear/logistic model coefficients: after standardizing features, coefficient magnitude reflects feature importance. Sign indicates direction of effect. Limitation: only valid for linear relationships; misleading when features are correlated with each other.

Decision tree paths: the tree structure itself is interpretable — each root-to-leaf path is an explicit if-then rule. Interpretability degrades as tree depth increases.

Random forest feature importance (MDI): mean decrease in impurity when a feature is used to split across all trees. Fast to compute. Biased toward high-cardinality numerical features. Unreliable when features are correlated.

Permutation importance: shuffle one feature column at random and measure the drop in validation performance. Model-agnostic. Less biased than MDI. Must be computed on the validation set, not the training set.

SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations): game-theory-based attribution. Assigns each feature a contribution to each individual prediction. Satisfies consistency and local accuracy properties. Computationally expensive but preferred when accurate per-prediction explanations are needed.

LIME: approximates any model locally around a specific prediction with an interpretable linear model. Useful for debugging individual predictions. Less theoretically grounded than SHAP.

Correlation ≠ causation: a feature that correlates with the target in training data may not be causally related to it. Models trained on spurious correlations fail under distribution shift. Always ask: would this feature still correlate with the target in a real deployment scenario?

Teach interpretability as a form of error analysis, not as a nice-to-have.

documents.title: ML workflow and scikit-learn pipeline
documents.content:
ML WORKFLOW AND PIPELINE

The full workflow sequence (teach in this order):
problem definition → data collection → EDA → data cleaning → preprocessing → baseline model → training → evaluation → hyperparameter tuning → error analysis → final model → deployment awareness

Never skip EDA before modeling. Univariate distributions, pairwise correlations, class distribution, missing value patterns, and feature-target relationships all drive preprocessing decisions.

Scikit-learn Pipeline: chains preprocessing and modeling steps into a single object. The critical benefit: when used inside cross_val_score or GridSearchCV, transformers are fit only on the training fold — preventing leakage automatically.

Example:
  pipe = Pipeline([
    ('scaler', StandardScaler()),
    ('model', LogisticRegression())
  ])
  pipe.fit(X_train, y_train)
  pipe.predict(X_test)

When using a Pipeline with cross-validation, the scaler sees only the training fold on each iteration. Without Pipeline, fitting the scaler on the full dataset first causes leakage.

Reproducibility: always set random_state in train_test_split, model constructors, and cross-validation. Document: data version, model version, hyperparameters, evaluation metric, and score. Without this, results cannot be reproduced or compared.

Saving models: joblib.dump(pipe, 'model.pkl'). Always save the full Pipeline, not just the model — otherwise the preprocessing steps are lost and the model cannot be used in production.

The test set rule: touched exactly once, after all tuning is complete. Treat it as a proxy for real-world performance.

documents.title: Neural networks — introduction and bridge to deep learning
documents.content:
NEURAL NETWORKS — INTRODUCTION AND BRIDGE TO DEEP LEARNING

Teach neural networks as a generalization of logistic regression, not as a separate field.

Perceptron: one neuron. Computes weighted sum of inputs, applies an activation function. A single perceptron is logistic regression with a non-linear output. The insight: stacking perceptrons creates layers that learn intermediate representations.

Multilayer network (MLP): input layer → one or more hidden layers → output layer. Hidden layers learn progressively more abstract features. More layers = capacity to model more complex functions.

Activation functions:
- Sigmoid: squashes output to (0, 1). Use in output layer for binary classification. Gradient vanishes in deep networks — avoid in hidden layers.
- ReLU: max(0, x). Default for hidden layers. Computationally cheap, avoids gradient vanishing for moderate depths.
- Softmax: output layer for multi-class. Converts raw scores (logits) to a probability distribution that sums to 1.

Backpropagation intuition: compute the loss at the output, then propagate the error backward through layers using the chain rule. Each weight receives a gradient proportional to its contribution to the final error. This is gradient descent applied to nested functions — the depth just means more chain rule applications.

Training controls:
- Epoch: one full pass through the training dataset
- Batch size: number of samples per weight update step
- Learning rate: step size per gradient update

Overfitting in neural networks: more common than in classical ML due to large parameter counts. Remedies: dropout (randomly zero out neurons during training), early stopping (stop when validation loss rises), L2 weight regularization, data augmentation.

When to use deep learning vs. classical ML: classical ML (especially gradient boosting) outperforms neural networks on most tabular datasets. Deep learning wins on images, text, audio, and sequences — particularly with large datasets.

documents.title: Common ML misconceptions — flag and correct proactively
documents.content:
COMMON ML MISCONCEPTIONS — FLAG AND CORRECT PROACTIVELY

Do not wait for the learner to discover these. Surface them explicitly when introducing the relevant concept.

1. 'More data always helps': more data reduces variance but not bias. A systematically biased or mislabeled dataset does not improve with scale.

2. 'Higher accuracy = better model': accuracy is meaningless on imbalanced datasets. A model predicting the majority class always beats a real model in raw accuracy on skewed data.

3. 'Train on all the data to get the best model': without a held-out test set, you have no way to know if your model generalizes. Training on all data destroys your ability to evaluate it honestly.

4. 'Neural networks are always better': gradient boosting outperforms neural networks on most tabular datasets. Neural networks require more data, more compute, and are harder to interpret.

5. 'Feature engineering is unnecessary with deep learning': for structured/tabular data, feature engineering matters even with deep learning models.

6. 'Cross-validation replaces the test set': cross-validation estimates generalization error during development. A held-out test set measures it once at the end. They serve different purposes.

7. 'Tuning on the test set is fine if I only look once': every time you observe test performance and change the model in response, you are leaking the test set. The test set is touched exactly once, unconditionally.

8. 'Correlation means causation': a model trained on spurious correlations will fail under distribution shift. Always ask whether a feature would still predict the target in a real deployment scenario where the correlation might not hold.

9. 'The Kaggle-winning model is the right model for production': Kaggle solutions optimize one metric on a fixed dataset. Production requires interpretability, latency, robustness, and maintainability.

10. 'A larger coefficient in a linear model means the feature matters more': coefficient magnitude depends on the scale of the feature. A feature measured in thousands will have a tiny coefficient; one measured in single digits will have a large one for equal predictive power. Compare coefficients only after standardizing features.

11. 'The probability output of a classifier is the actual probability': most classifiers output scores, not calibrated probabilities. A model outputting 0.85 does not necessarily mean there is an 85% real-world chance of the positive class. Logistic regression is roughly calibrated; random forests and gradient boosting typically are not without post-hoc calibration.

documents.title: Minimum math coverage for ML foundations
documents.content:
MINIMUM MATH COVERAGE

Students do not need proof-heavy math, but they need enough to not treat models as magic.

Linear algebra:
- Vectors: a feature vector is a point in n-dimensional space; distance between points is similarity between samples
- Matrices: a dataset is a matrix where rows are samples and columns are features; model parameters form another matrix
- Dot product: the core operation in linear regression, logistic regression, SVM, and every neural network layer
- Matrix multiplication: how neural network layers transform their input

Probability:
- Distributions: a classifier outputs a probability distribution over classes
- Conditional probability: P(class|features) is what every supervised classifier estimates
- Bayes theorem: posterior ∝ likelihood × prior; the foundation of Naive Bayes and probabilistic model interpretation
- Expected value and variance: basis of bias-variance tradeoff language

Statistics:
- Mean, variance, standard deviation: basis for feature scaling and understanding distributions
- Correlation: linear relationship between features and targets; not causation
- Sampling: why random splits matter; why stratified sampling preserves class ratios

Calculus (for optimization topics):
- Derivative: rate of change — how much does the loss change per unit change in a weight?
- Gradient: the multivariate derivative — the direction of steepest ascent in parameter space
- Chain rule: how error propagates backward through nested functions (backpropagation)
- Optimization intuition: minimizing loss by moving opposite to the gradient

Treatment level: geometric and intuitive explanations are sufficient. Full algebraic derivations are not required at the foundational level.

documents.title: Production awareness — distribution shift and model degradation
documents.content:
PRODUCTION AWARENESS

A model that passes evaluation does not always work in production. Every ML learner should understand why, at a conceptual level.

Distribution shift: ML models are trained on historical data. When the statistical properties of real-world inputs change from what the model was trained on, performance degrades. This is called distribution shift or data drift. It is expected, not a rare failure.

Types of drift:
- Covariate shift: the distribution of input features changes (new customer demographics, seasonal changes in usage), but the relationship between features and the target stays the same.
- Concept drift: the underlying relationship between inputs and output changes — the pattern the model learned is no longer valid. A fraud model trained on 2022 patterns may be blind to 2024 fraud tactics.
- Label drift: the distribution of the target variable itself changes (more positives, fewer negatives).

Scale of the problem: a landmark study found 91% of deployed ML models degrade in performance over time due to data drift. Retraining on fresh data and monitoring production metrics are standard industry practices, not advanced topics.

Temporal leakage (for sequential data): any dataset with a time dimension must be split by time, not randomly. A random split allows future records into the training set, creating leakage that produces optimistic evaluation scores and poor production performance.

Problem definition first: before building any model, the minimum questions are — what decision does this model enable, what are the consequences of wrong predictions, and is ML even the right tool? Rule-based systems, lookup tables, or simpler statistical approaches often outperform ML when data is scarce or the pattern is stable. This question is systematically skipped in beginner courses.
